volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Trojan Women (Section 1 - Section 1.175)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: Ἥκω λιπὼν Αἴγαιον ἁλμυρὸν βάθος
sections.translation: From the depths of salt Aegean floods I , Poseidon , have come , where choirs of Nereids dance in a graceful maze ; for since the day that Phoebus and I with exact measurement

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: Φοῖβός τε κἀγὼ λαΐνους πύργους πέριξ
sections.translation: set towers of stone about this land of Troy and ringed it round , never from my heart has passed away a kindly feeling for my Phrygian town , which now is smouldering and overthrown , a prey to Argive might . For , from his home beneath Parnassus ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: Φωκεὺς Ἐπειός , μηχαναῖσι Παλλάδος
sections.translation: Phocian Epeus , aided by the craft of Pallas , framed a horse to bear within its womb an armed army , and sent it within the battlements , a deadly statue ; from which in days to come men shall tell of the Wooden Horse , with its hidden load of warriors .

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ἔρημα δʼ ἄλση καὶ θεῶν ἀνάκτορα
sections.translation: Groves stand forsaken and temples of the gods run down with blood , and at the altar ’ s very base , before the god who watched his home , Priam lies dead . While to Achaean ships great store of gold and Phrygian spoils are being conveyed ,

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: πρύμνηθεν οὖρον , ὡς δεκασπόρῳ χρόνῳ
sections.translation: and they who came against this town , those sons of Hellas , only wait a favoring breeze to follow in their wake , that after ten long years they may with joy behold their wives and children . Vanquished by Hera , Argive goddess , and by Athena , who helped to ruin Phrygia ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: λείπω τὸ κλεινὸν Ἴλιον βωμούς τʼ ἐμούς ·
sections.translation: I am leaving Ilium , that famous town , and my altars ; for when dreary desolation seizes on a town , the worship of the gods decays and tends to lose respect . Scamander ’ s banks re-echo long and loud the screams of captive maids , as they by lot receive their masters .

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: καὶ τὰς μὲν Ἀρκάς , τὰς δὲ Θεσσαλὸς λεὼς
sections.translation: Arcadia takes some , and some the people of Thessaly ; others are assigned to Theseus ’ sons , the Athenian chiefs . And such of the Trojan women as are not portioned out are in these tents , set apart for the leaders of the army ; and with them Spartan Helen ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: Ἑλένη , νομισθεῖσʼ αἰχμάλωτος ἐνδίκως .
sections.translation: daughter of Tyndareus , justly counted among the captives . And if you would see that queen of misery , Hecuba , you can ; for there she lies before the gates , weeping many tears for many sorrows ; at Achilles ’ tomb ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: λάθρα τέθνηκε τλημόνως Πολυξένη ·
sections.translation: without her knowledge , her daughter Polyxena has died most piteously ; Priam is gone , and her children too ; Cassandra , whom the lord Apollo left to be a virgin , frenzied maid , has been forced by Agamemnon , in contempt of the god ’ s ordinance and of piety , to a dishonored wedlock .

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , ὦ ποτʼ εὐτυχοῦσα , χαῖρέ μοι , πόλις
sections.translation: Farewell , O city once prosperous ! farewell , you ramparts of polished stone ! if Pallas , daughter of Zeus , had not decreed your ruin , you would be standing firmly still .

sections.label: Section 48
sections.original: ἔξεστι τὸν γένει μὲν ἄγχιστον πατρὸς
sections.translation: May I address the mighty god whom the gods revere and who to my own father is very near in blood ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: λύσασαν ἔχθραν τὴν πάρος , προσεννέπειν ;
sections.translation: laying aside our former enmity ?

sections.label: Section 51
sections.original: ἔξεστιν · αἱ γὰρ συγγενεῖς ὁμιλίαι ,
sections.translation: You may ; for over the soul the ties of kin exert no feeble spell , great queen Athena .

sections.label: Section 53
sections.original: ἐπῄνεσʼ ὀργὰς ἠπίους · φέρω δὲ σοὶ
sections.translation: For your forgiving mood my thanks ! I have messages to impart affecting both yourself and me , lord .

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: μῶν ἐκ θεῶν του καινὸν ἀγγελεῖς ἔπος ,
sections.translation: Do you bring fresh tidings from some god , from Zeus , or from some lesser power ?

sections.label: Section 57
sections.original: οὔκ , ἀλλὰ Τροίας οὕνεκʼ , ἔνθα βαίνομεν ,
sections.translation: From none of these ; but on behalf of Troy , whose soil we tread , I have come to seek your mighty aid , to make it one with mine .

sections.label: Section 59
sections.original: ἦ πού νιν , ἔχθραν τὴν πρὶν ἐκβαλοῦσα , νῦν
sections.translation: What ! have you laid your former hate aside

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: ἐς οἶκτον ἦλθες πυρὶ κατῃθαλωμένης ;
sections.translation: to take compassion on the town now that it is burnt to ashes ?

sections.label: Section 61
sections.original: ἐκεῖσε πρῶτʼ ἄνελθε · κοινώσῃ λόγους
sections.translation: First go back to the former point ; will you make common cause with me in the scheme I purpose ?

sections.label: Section 63
sections.original: μάλιστʼ · ἀτὰρ δὴ καὶ τὸ σὸν θέλω μαθεῖν ·
sections.translation: Yes , surely ; but I want to learn your wishes , whether you have come to help Achaeans or Phrygians .

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: τοὺς μὲν πρὶν ἐχθροὺς Τρῶας εὐφρᾶναι θέλω ,
sections.translation: I wish to give my former foes , the Trojans , joy , and on the Achaean army impose a bitter return .

sections.label: Section 67
sections.original: τί δʼ ὧδε πηδᾷς ἄλλοτʼ εἰς ἄλλους τρόπους
sections.translation: Why do you leap thus from mood to mood ? Your love and hate both go too far , on whomever centred .

sections.label: Section 69
sections.original: οὐκ οἶσθʼ ὑβρισθεῖσάν με καὶ ναοὺς ἐμούς ;
sections.translation: Do you not know the insult done to me and to the shrine I love ?

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: οἶδʼ , ἡνίκʼ Αἴας εἷλκε Κασάνδραν βίᾳ .
sections.translation: I do : when Aias dragged away Cassandra by force .

sections.label: Section 71
sections.original: κοὐδέν γʼ Ἀχαιῶν ἔπαθεν οὐδʼ ἤκουσʼ ὕπο .
sections.translation: Yes , and the Achaeans did nothing , said nothing to him .

sections.label: Section 72
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ἔπερσάν γʼ Ἴλιον τῷ σῷ σθένει .
sections.translation: And yet it was by your mighty aid they sacked Ilium .

sections.label: Section 73
sections.original: τοιγάρ σφε σὺν σοὶ βούλομαι δρᾶσαι κακῶς .
sections.translation: For which cause I would join with you to do them harm .

sections.label: Section 74
sections.original: ἕτοιμʼ ἃ βούλῃ τἀπʼ ἐμοῦ . δράσεις δὲ τί ;
sections.translation: My powers are ready at your will . What is your intent ?

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: δύσνοστον αὐτοῖς νόστον ἐμβαλεῖν θέλω .
sections.translation: I will impose on them a return that is no return .

sections.label: Section 76
sections.original: ἐν γῇ μενόντων ἢ καθʼ ἁλμυρὰν ἅλα ;
sections.translation: While they stay on shore , or as they cross the salt sea ?

sections.label: Section 77
sections.original: ὅταν πρὸς οἴκους ναυστολῶσʼ ἀπʼ Ἰλίου .
sections.translation: When they have set sail from Ilium for their homes . On them will Zeus also send his rain and fearful hail ,

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: ἐμοὶ δὲ δώσειν φησὶ πῦρ κεραύνιον ,
sections.translation: and inky tempests from the sky ; and he promises to grant me his thunder-bolts to hurl on the Achaeans and fire their ships . And you , for your part , make the Aegean strait to roar with mighty billows and whirlpools , and fill Euboea ’ s hollow bay with corpses ,

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: ὡς ἂν τὸ λοιπὸν τἄμʼ ἀνάκτορʼ εὐσεβεῖν
sections.translation: that Achaeans may learn henceforth to reverence my temples and regard all other deities .

sections.label: Section 87
sections.original: ἔσται τάδʼ · ἡ χάρις γὰρ οὐ μακρῶν λόγων
sections.translation: So shall it be , for this favor needs only a few words . I will vex the broad Aegean sea ; and the beach of Myconos and the reefs round Delos ,

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: Σκῦρός τε Λῆμνός θʼ αἱ Καφήρειοί τʼ ἄκραι
sections.translation: Scyros and Lemnos too , and the cliffs of Caphareus shall be strewn with many a corpse . You go to Olympus , and taking from your father ’ s hand his lightning bolts , keep careful watch against the hour when Argos ’ army lets slip its cables .

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: μῶρος δὲ θνητῶν ὅστις ἐκπορθεῖ πόλεις ,
sections.translation: A fool is he who sacks the towns of men , with shrines and tombs , the dead man ’ s hallowed home , for at the last he makes a desert round himself and dies .

sections.label: Section 98
sections.original: ἄνα , δύσδαιμον , πεδόθεν κεφαλή ·
sections.translation: Lift your head , unhappy one , from the ground ; raise up your neck ; this is Troy no more ,

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: τάδε καὶ βασιλῆς ἐσμεν Τροίας .
sections.translation: no longer am I queen in Ilium . Though fortune change , endure your lot ; sail with the stream , and follow fortune ’ s tack , do not steer your ship of life against the tide , since chance must guide your course .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: αἰαῖ αἰαῖ .
sections.translation: Ah me ! ah me ! What else but tears is now my hapless lot , whose country , children , husband , all are lost ? Ah ! the high-blown pride of ancestors , humbled ! how brought to nothing after all !

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: τί με χρὴ σιγᾶν ; τί δὲ μὴ σιγᾶν ;
sections.translation: What woe must I suppress , or what declare ? What plaintive dirge shall I awake ? Ah , woe is me ! the anguish I suffer lying here stretched upon this hard pallet !

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: οἴμοι κεφαλῆς , οἴμοι κροτάφων
sections.translation: O my head , my temples , my side ! How I long to turn over , and lie now on this , now on that , to rest my back and spine , while ceaselessly my tearful wail ascends .

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: μοῦσα δὲ χαὕτη τοῖς δυστήνοις
sections.translation: For even this is music to the wretched , to chant their cheerless dirge of sorrow .

sections.label: Section 122
sections.original: πρῷραι ναῶν , ὠκείαις
sections.translation: You swift-prowed ships , rowed to sacred Ilium over the deep dark sea ,

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: λιμένας Ἑλλάδος εὐόρμους
sections.translation: past the fair havens of Hellas , to the flute ’ s ill-omened music and the dulcet voice of pipes ,

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: αἰαῖ , Τροίας ἐν κόλποις
sections.translation: to the bays of Troy , alas ! where you tied your hawsers , twisted handiwork from Egypt , in quest of that hateful wife of Menelaus , who brought disgrace on Castor , and on Eurotas foul reproach ; who murdered

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: τὸν πεντήκοντʼ ἀροτῆρα τέκνων
sections.translation: Priam , the father of fifty children ; the cause why I , the unhappy Hecuba , have wrecked my life upon this disastrous strand . Oh that I should sit here , over against the tent of Agamemnon !

sections.label: Section 140
sections.original: δούλα δʼ ἄγομαι
sections.translation: As a slave I am led away from my home , an old woman , while from my head the hair is piteously shorn for grief . Ah ! unhappy wives of those armored sons of Troy ! Ah ! poor maidens , luckless brides ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: τύφεται Ἴλιον , αἰάζωμεν .
sections.translation: come weep , for Ilium is now a smouldering ruin ; and I , like some mother-bird that over her fledgelings screams , will begin the strain ; not the same as that

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: σκήπτρῳ Πριάμου διερειδομένα
sections.translation: I once sang to the gods , as I leaned on Priam ’ s staff and beat with my foot in Phrygian time to lead the dance !

sections.label: Section 1.153
sections.original: Ἑκάβη , τί θροεῖς ; τί δὲ θωΰσσεις ;
sections.translation: O Hecuba ! why these cries , these piercing shrieks ? What do your words mean ? For I heard your piteous wail

sections.label: Section 1.155
sections.original: ἄιον οἴκτους οὓς οἰκτίζῃ .
sections.translation: echo through the building , and a pang of terror shoots through each captive Trojan ’ s breast , as within these walls they mourn their slavish lot .

sections.label: Section 1.159
sections.original: ὦ τέκνʼ , Ἀργείων πρὸς ναῦς ἤδη ⋯
sections.translation: My child , even now at the ships of the Argives —

sections.label: Section 1.160
sections.original: κινεῖται κωπήρης χείρ ;
sections.translation: [*] The rower ’ s hand is busy ?

sections.label: Section 1.161
sections.original: οἲ ἐγώ , τί θέλουσʼ , ἦ πού μʼ ἤδη
sections.translation: Ah , woe is me ! what is their intent ? Will they really carry me away from my country in their fleet ?

sections.label: Section 1.163
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ , εἰκάζω δʼ ἄταν .
sections.translation: I do not know , though I guess our doom .

sections.label: Section 1.164
sections.original: ἰὼ ἰώ .
sections.translation: O misery !

sections.label: Section 1.165
sections.original: μέλεαι μόχθων ἐπακουσόμεναι
sections.translation: woe to us Trojan women , soon to hear of our troubles : Come out of the house , the Argives are preparing to return .

sections.label: Section 1.168
sections.original: αἶ , αἶ .
sections.translation: Oh ! please do not bid the

sections.label: Section 1.170
sections.original: ἐκβακχεύουσαν Κασάνδραν ,
sections.translation: wild Cassandra leave her chamber , the frantic prophetess , for Argives to insult , nor to my griefs add yet another . Woe to you , ill-fated Troy , Troy , your sun is set ; and woe to your unhappy children , living and dead alike ,

sections.label: Section 1.175
sections.original: καὶ ζῶντες καὶ δμαθέντες .
sections.translation: who are leaving you behind !
